summary:
Umm Dabaghiyah is a small Neolithic site in northwestern Iraq, dated to approximately 6000 BCE and excavated by Diana Kirkbride in the early 1970s. The site is notable for being one of the few Neolithic settlements in northern Mesopotamia that appears to have had a specialized economic function rather than being a generalized farming village — specifically, it shows evidence of large-scale hunting and processing of the onager (wild ass) and other steppe animals, alongside storage facilities that may have served a redistributive function.

The unusual economic character of Umm Dabaghiyah, with its emphasis on hunting and its large storage buildings, has led some archaeologists to interpret it as a specialized hunting camp or a site where steppe animal products were accumulated for exchange with other communities. This interpretation, if correct, would make it evidence of economic specialization and inter-community exchange in northern Mesopotamia at a very early date — earlier than such specialization was previously recognized.

The site's painted pottery, related to but distinct from the [[event:1403|Hassuna culture]] tradition of the region, features unusual friezes of onager heads and hunting scenes — one of the very few examples of painted figurative art on early Mesopotamian pottery. These painted scenes, combined with the large quantities of onager bones at the site, provide a vivid glimpse into the specialized activities and artistic traditions of this early Neolithic community on the margins of the Mesopotamian steppe.
